hero.title: Tools we choose for a reason.
hero.lead: We don't chase trends. Every tool in our stack solves a specific problem — performance, reliability, or speed of delivery. Here's what we use and why.

categories.name: Frontend & Interfaces
categories.description: Building operational interfaces that teams actually want to use — fast, reliable, and clear under pressure.

tools.name: React 18
tools.benefit: Component-based architecture lets us build complex operational UIs that stay maintainable as requirements evolve. The ecosystem gives us battle-tested solutions for real-time data, maps, and dashboards.
tools.useCase: All client-facing operational dashboards, control panels, and exception queues.

tools.name: TypeScript
tools.benefit: Operational systems can't afford type errors at runtime. TypeScript catches entire classes of bugs before they reach production — especially important when multiple engineers work on the same codebase.
tools.useCase: Every frontend and backend codebase. Non-negotiable for new projects.

tools.name: Tailwind CSS
tools.benefit: Utility-first means we never fight CSS specificity or worry about breaking unrelated components. Operational teams need consistent, predictable interfaces — Tailwind delivers that without overhead.
tools.useCase: All frontend projects. Enables rapid iteration without CSS debt.

tools.name: React Query
tools.benefit: Server state is the hard part of operational UIs — not rendering. React Query handles caching, background updates, and stale data invalidation so engineers focus on what matters.
tools.useCase: Data-heavy dashboards and real-time operational views.

categories.name: Backend & APIs
categories.description: Systems that stay reliable when it matters. We prioritize boring technology that works predictably at scale.

tools.name: Node.js + Express
tools.benefit: JavaScript across the stack reduces context switching. Express is unopinionated enough to let us structure systems the way operational logic demands — not the way a framework dictates.
tools.useCase: API services, real-time endpoints, and integration layers.

tools.name: Python FastAPI
tools.benefit: Data-heavy operations and ML pipelines belong in Python. FastAPI gives us automatic OpenAPI docs, async support, and type validation — perfect for internal tools and data services.
tools.useCase: Data pipelines, ETL processes, and analytical services.

tools.name: PostgreSQL
tools.benefit: Reliability and data integrity aren't optional in operational systems. PostgreSQL gives us transactional guarantees, JSON support, and battle-tested performance under load.
tools.useCase: Primary data store for all client platforms.

tools.name: Redis
tools.benefit: Operational decisions need sub-millisecond latency for caches, rate limiting, and real-time queues. Redis delivers that without complexity.
tools.useCase: Session storage, rate limiting, and real-time job queues.

categories.name: Data & Streaming
categories.description: Real-time data is the difference between reactive and proactive operations. These tools keep information flowing.

tools.name: Apache Kafka
tools.benefit: Operational visibility depends on event-driven architecture. Kafka decouples data producers from consumers, so telemetry never blocks operations and failures don't cascade.
tools.useCase: IoT telemetry ingestion, event sourcing, and audit logging.

tools.name: dbt
tools.benefit: Trustworthy data needs repeatable transformations. dbt brings software engineering practices (testing, documentation, version control) to SQL — so analysts and engineers collaborate effectively.
tools.useCase: Data warehousing, reporting pipelines, and business metrics.

tools.name: Apache Airflow
tools.benefit: Scheduled data pipelines need visibility and retry logic. Airflow gives us DAG-based orchestration with built-in monitoring — critical for ETL that operations teams depend on.
tools.useCase: Daily reporting, data syncs, and batch processing.

categories.name: Infrastructure & DevOps
categories.description: Infrastructure as code, because manual configuration fails silently. Every environment is reproducible and auditable.

tools.name: AWS
tools.benefit: Mature services across compute, storage, and networking. We use what fits the problem — EC2 for predictable workloads, Lambda for event-driven functions, and managed services where they reduce operational burden.
tools.useCase: Primary cloud provider for all client platforms.

tools.name: Terraform
tools.benefit: Infrastructure as code means no undocumented changes, no configuration drift, and full audit trails. Terraform lets us review infrastructure changes like code — because they are code.
tools.useCase: All infrastructure provisioning across environments.

tools.name: Docker + ECS
tools.benefit: Containerization eliminates 'works on my machine' problems. ECS gives us orchestration without managing Kubernetes control planes — simpler for the scale we operate at.
tools.useCase: Container runtime and orchestration for all services.

tools.name: GitHub Actions
tools.benefit: CI/CD integrated with code review means deployment pipelines aren't separate from development. Every PR triggers tests; every merge to main deploys when tests pass.
tools.useCase: Automated testing, building, and deployment.

categories.name: Observability & Monitoring
categories.description: If it's not measured, it's not working. We instrument everything because operational systems fail in unexpected ways.

tools.name: Datadog
tools.benefit: Unified platform for metrics, logs, and traces. When an alert fires, we have context — not just a notification. This matters when a client's operation is waiting on us.
tools.useCase: Production monitoring, alerting, and performance analysis.

tools.name: Sentry
tools.benefit: Frontend errors reach users even when backend is fine. Sentry captures client-side exceptions with context — browser, user action, network state — so we fix what actually broke.
tools.useCase: Error tracking and performance monitoring for frontend apps.

tools.name: Logstash + OpenSearch
tools.benefit: Centralized logging with full-text search. When debugging distributed systems, being able to trace a request across services is non-negotiable.
tools.useCase: Log aggregation and search across all services.

categories.name: Team & Collaboration
categories.description: Tools that reduce friction, not add overhead. We choose what helps us communicate clearly.

tools.name: Linear
tools.benefit: Issue tracking designed for speed. Keyboard-first, no unnecessary fields, and cycles instead of arbitrary deadlines. It matches how we actually work.
tools.useCase: Project management, issue tracking, and sprint planning.

tools.name: Slack
tools.benefit: Synchronous communication when needed, asynchronous by default. Integrations with Linear, GitHub, and Datadog mean alerts and updates come to us.
tools.useCase: Team communication and integrations.

tools.name: Notion
tools.benefit: Documentation that's actually used. Notion lets us embed databases, code blocks, and diagrams in the same place — so operational runbooks and architecture docs stay up to date.
tools.useCase: Documentation, runbooks, and knowledge base.

tools.name: Miro
tools.benefit: Whiteboarding for distributed teams. Architecture reviews, incident post-mortems, and operational process mapping — visual collaboration without being in the same room.
tools.useCase: Diagramming, brainstorming, and process mapping.

principles.title: How we evaluate new tools
principles.description: We don't add tools without answering these questions first. Every addition to our stack needs to justify itself.

items.title: Does it solve a real problem?
items.description: We don't adopt tools because they're popular. We adopt them because they fix something we're actually struggling with — and the alternatives don't.

items.title: Can we operate it reliably?
items.description: Bleeding-edge tools that require constant attention take focus away from client work. We prioritize boring technology that works predictably.

items.title: Does it reduce cognitive load?
items.description: Every tool adds mental overhead. If a tool doesn't make the team's life noticeably easier, it doesn't belong in our stack.

items.title: Can clients access it?
items.description: We avoid proprietary tools that lock clients in. If a client needs access to logs or metrics, we use tools that support external access.

cta.title: Interested in how we build?
cta.description: We're always open to conversations with engineers who care about tooling and architecture. If you have thoughts on our stack — or questions about why we chose what we did — reach out.
